planting.note: Sow direct in fall after scarifying seed coat with sandpaper and allowing natural winter cold stratification; wild blue indigo is extremely slow from seed — expect 3–5 years before first bloom — but becomes a massive, long-lived, drought-tolerant prairie cornerstone once established.

directions: Sow direct in fall after scarifying the hard seed coat with sandpaper or nicking it with a knife, then pressing seeds into the surface of lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; seeds cold-stratify naturally over winter and germinate in spring — transplanting is not recommended as wild blue indigo develops a very deep, extensive taproot that resents disturbance. Baptisia australis is one of the most spectacular and long-lived native perennials of the eastern tallgrass prairie and open woodland edges, producing stout, blue-green, glaucous stems bearing trifoliate leaves and dense, upright racemes of deep indigo-blue, pea-like flowers from April through June that are highly attractive to native bumblebees and large carpenter bees. After flowering, the inflated, charcoal-black seed pods are persistent and ornamentally valuable through fall and winter, rattling audibly in the wind; the entire plant turns charcoal-black at frost and can be left standing for winter interest and wildlife cover. Wild blue indigo is an exceptional prairie restoration plant and a nitrogen-fixer; once established after its slow juvenile period, it is essentially indestructible and may live for decades, forming clumps 4 feet wide and wide — do not move or divide established plants.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in average to lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Ohio spiderwort spreads by self-sowing and forms expanding clumps — divide every 2–3 years in spring to control spread and rejuvenate vigor.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in average to lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Ohio spiderwort is adaptable and drought-tolerant once established, performing well in dry to average soils and tolerating clay — rich, fertile soils encourage excessive flopping. Tradescantia ohiensis is a native prairie and woodland-edge perennial of the central and eastern United States producing upright, glaucous, blue-green stems bearing long, strap-like leaves and terminal clusters of bright blue-violet, three-petaled flowers from April through June; each individual flower lasts only a single morning, opening at dawn and wilting by noon, but the plant produces a long succession of blooms over several weeks. It is a significant early-season pollen source for native bumblebees, mason bees, and specialist Tradescantia-foraging sweat bees; the flowers are visited almost exclusively in the morning hours when they are open. Ohio spiderwort goes summer-dormant in hot, dry weather, cutting back naturally in July — this is normal; plant it among later-emerging perennials to fill the gap, and it will re-emerge and often rebloom in the cool of early fall.

planting.note: Sow direct in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; nettle-leaf sage is one of the most shade-tolerant native Salvia species in eastern North America and is best suited to woodland edge and dappled-shade settings.

directions: Sow direct in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; seeds benefit from natural cold stratification over winter and germinate in spring — nettle-leaf sage adapts to the kind of moderately moist, shaded woodland floor where most sun-loving sages fail. Salvia urticifolia is a uncommon and underused native perennial sage of the southeastern Appalachians and adjacent piedmont, native to moist, shaded slopes, woodland edges, and stream margins; it is distinguished by its large, nettle-like, coarsely toothed, aromatic leaves (urticifolia = nettle-leaved) that give it a lush, bold texture in shaded garden settings. It produces upright stems bearing whorls of tubular, blue to blue-purple flowers from May through July that are attractive to native bumblebees and hummingbirds — a notable distinction in a genus where most species require full sun. Nettle-leaf sage is an excellent choice for shaded native plant gardens, woodland edges, and naturalistic plantings in the mid-Atlantic and upper South; it self-sows modestly and is not aggressively spreading.

planting.note: Sow direct in fall after scarifying the hard seed coat with sandpaper; wild senna seeds have impermeable seed coats that require scarification for reliable germination — fall sowing after scarification allows cold-stratification over winter and strong germination in spring.

directions: Sow direct in fall after scarifying the hard seed coat with sandpaper or soaking in hot water for 24 hours; wild senna has a physical seed dormancy due to an impermeable seed coat that must be broken before water can enter and germination can proceed — without scarification, germination rates are very poor. Senna marilandica is a bold, tall native perennial of eastern North American prairies, meadows, and open woodland edges producing pinnately compound, fine-textured leaves on robust stems and clusters of bright yellow, five-petaled flowers from July through August that are exceptional for pollinators — it is a host plant for several native sulfur butterflies and cloudless sulphur, and the flowers are heavily visited by bumblebees harvesting pollen. It is a nitrogen-fixing legume, improving soil fertility over time; established plants develop a deep taproot and become very drought-tolerant despite their large size, though they perform best with moderate moisture during establishment. Wild senna spreads moderately by self-sowing and by short rhizomes; the persistent, straight, flattened seed pods provide wildlife food and winter garden interest — it is an ideal structural plant for the back of a native border or meadow planting.

planting.note: Sow direct after last frost in warm, well-drained to dry, lean soil in full sun; sicklepod is a warm-season annual that germinates quickly in hot soil and is well adapted to disturbed, dry, open ground across the southeastern United States.

directions: Sow direct after the last frost date when soil has warmed to at least 70°F in well-drained to dry, lean soil in full sun; sicklepod germinates rapidly in hot conditions and is not suited to heavy clay or consistently wet soils — it thrives in the kind of dry, open, disturbed ground where many other plants struggle. Senna obtusifolia is a native warm-season annual of the southeastern United States and Gulf Coast, producing pinnately compound leaves with broadly obovate leaflets and paired, bright yellow, five-petaled flowers from June through September; its common name refers to the distinctive curved, sickle-shaped seed pods that follow the flowers and are a key identification feature. The roasted seeds have a long history of use as a coffee substitute — known as 'java bean' or 'foetid cassia coffee' — and the young leaves are used as a food vegetable in parts of West Africa, where the plant has naturalized extensively; it is also an important host plant for cloudless sulphur and other native sulfur butterflies. Sicklepod self-sows prolifically in warm climates and can naturalize in open sandy or disturbed ground; it is well suited to naturalistic plantings, butterfly gardens, and dry roadsides in the Deep South.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Culver's root is a tall, elegant native prairie perennial that establishes readily from division and rewards patience with one of the most refined flower spikes in the native plant palette.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Culver's root adapts to average garden soils with consistent moisture but performs best in rich, moist conditions and tolerates partial shade better than most tall prairie perennials. Veronicastrum virginicum is one of the most architecturally elegant native perennials of the eastern tallgrass prairie, producing tall, straight, unbranched stems with distinctive whorled leaves arranged in tiers of 3–7 and slender, candelabra-like spikes of tiny, tubular, white to pale lavender flowers from July through August; the multiple flower spikes arising from a single stem create a striking, tiered silhouette that is unlike any other native prairie plant. It is an exceptional native bee plant — the flowers support an extraordinary diversity of specialist and generalist native bees, including long-tongued bumblebees, sweat bees, and mining bees — and is consistently rated among the top native prairie perennials for pollinator value per square foot. Culver's root is slow to establish from seed but long-lived once settled; it naturalizes well in prairie restorations, tall native borders, and rain garden edges, and the dried seed heads provide winter structure and wildlife food — it should not be confused with the related non-native Veronica species.
